terms.definition: An object representing a file system path (location) to a file or directory.
Legacy class representing file and directory pathnames; often used with Scanner or to check existence.
terms.example: File f = new File("output.txt"); boolean exists = f.exists();

terms.definition: A text parser that reads tokens or lines from an input source such as a File or InputStream.
terms.example: Scanner sc = new Scanner(new File("nums.txt")); int n = sc.nextInt();

terms.definition: A character stream for reading text from files; often wrapped by BufferedReader.
Adds buffering and convenient readLine() to another Reader for efficient text input.
terms.example: BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("in.txt")); String line = br.readLine();

terms.definition: A character stream for writing text to files; often wrapped by BufferedWriter or PrintWriter.
A writer with convenient print/println/printf methods for formatted text output.
Utility class (java.nio.file) with static methods for common file operations.
Factory class for creating Path instances from strings or URI.
Language construct that automatically closes resources (e.g., Readers, Writers) when the block exits.
terms.example: try (Scanner sc = new Scanner(new File("in.txt"))) { /* use sc */ }

terms.definition: A checked exception signaling a general I/O error (read/write/close failures).
A checked exception thrown when a file cannot be found when opening for read/write.
terms.example: new Scanner(new File("missing.txt")); // may throw FileNotFoundException

terms.definition: A character or pattern that separates tokens when parsing text.
A common Unicode text encoding; recommended default for cross-platform text files.
The newline sequence used to end a line (e.g., \n on Unix).
terms.example: pw.println("First line"); // writes a line separator
